customer_name: Vermont Museum of Natural History Inc.

citations.narrative:
Records have not been updated to reflect recent acquisitions and a disposition of animals at the facility. A skunk and an
opossum had recently been acquired, however records had not been updated to include the addition of the animals. In
addition, records did not reflect the date of disposition of one opossum. Inventory records must be kept current and
maintained in order to accurately reflect the animals housed at the facility. The facility must correct this issue.
******Correct by February 25, 2026

The facility has a documented contingency plan, however an annual review has not been conducted since May of 2024.
An annual review of the contingency plan helps ensure that any needed changes are made and communicated to all
personnel that have a role or responsibilities in the plan to provide for the humane handling, treatment, transportation,
housing and care of the animals in the event of an emergency or disaster. The facility must ensure that the contingency
plan is reviewed on at least an annual basis and must also maintain documentation of those reviews. The facility must
correct this issue.
********Correct by March 1, 2026

The freezer which was used to store bird food items was observed. The facility was also storing leftover food items which
had been collected from the bird enclosures as part of the process in order to monitor consumption. This consisted of a
bag of leftover food items which contained feathers and waste debris. Animal waste and food residue can potentially lead
to contamination of the animals diet, and these items not intended for food must not be kept in food storage areas or food
freezers. The facility must correct this issue.
*******Correct by February 24, 2026

The broad- winged hawk and turkey vulture are brought indoors during times of extremely low temperatures, however
permanent indoor enclosures are not available for the birds. When night temperatures are predicted to fall below ten
degrees F, the birds are held overnight indoors in standard transport enclosures, being brought indoors at the end of the
day, and removed from the transport enclosures in the morning when the staff arrive to take them to their primary
enclosures. While the transport enclosures meet the regulatory requirements for transport the birds are being housed, not
in transport. Transport enclosures do not provide enough space for the birds to make normal postural adjustments.
Inadequate space can cause distress to the birds, and can lead to negative health impacts. The facility must ensure all
animals are housed in enclosures which provide adequate space for the animals to make normal postural adjustments.
The facility must correct this issue.
********Correct by March 1, 2026
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the facility representatives.
n

narrative:
2.75(b)(1) 
   
Records: Dealers and exhibitors. 
Records have not been updated to reflect recent acquisitions and a disposition of animals at the facility. A skunk and an 
opossum had recently been acquired, however records had not been updated to include the addition of the animals. In 
addition, records did not reflect the date of disposition of one opossum. Inventory records must be kept current and 
maintained in order to accurately reflect the animals housed at the facility. The facility must correct this issue. 
******Correct by February 25, 2026 
2.134(b) 
   
Contingency planning. 
The facility has a documented contingency plan, however an annual review has not been conducted since May of 2024. 
An annual review of the contingency plan helps ensure that any needed changes are made and communicated to all 
personnel that have a role or responsibilities in the plan to provide for the humane handling, treatment, transportation, 
housing and care of the animals in the event of an emergency or disaster. The facility must ensure that the contingency 
plan is reviewed on at least an annual basis and must also maintain documentation of those reviews. The facility must 
correct this issue.  
 
********Correct by March 1, 2026 
3.150(e) 
   
Facilities, general. 
The freezer which was used to store bird food items was observed. The facility was also storing leftover food items which 
had been collected from the bird enclosures as part of the process in order to monitor consumption. This consisted of a 
bag of leftover food items which contained feathers and waste debris. Animal waste and food residue can potentially lead 
to contamination of the animals diet, and these items not intended for food must not be kept in food storage areas or food 
freezers. The facility must correct this issue. 
 
*******Correct by February 24, 2026

3.153(b) 
   
Primary enclosures. 
The broad- winged hawk and turkey vulture are brought indoors during times of extremely low temperatures, however 
permanent indoor enclosures are not available for the birds. When night temperatures are predicted to fall below ten 
degrees F, the birds are held overnight indoors in standard transport enclosures, being brought indoors at the end of the 
day, and removed from the transport enclosures in the morning when the staff arrive to take them to their primary 
enclosures. While the transport enclosures meet the regulatory requirements for transport the birds are being housed, not 
in transport. Transport enclosures do not provide enough space for the birds to make normal postural adjustments. 
Inadequate space can cause distress to the birds, and can lead to negative health impacts. The facility must ensure all 
animals are housed in enclosures which provide adequate space for the animals to make normal postural adjustments. 
The facility must correct this issue.  
 
********Correct by March 1, 2026 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the facility representatives.
